findings.description: Client identity is taken from the leftmost, fully attacker-controlled value of X-Forwarded-For (CWE-290/CWE-348); an attacker can rotate this header per request to get a fresh bucket and completely bypass the rate limit, or set it to a victim's IP to throttle that victim — trust the rightmost proxy-appended hop or Remote-Addr instead.
Off-by-one in the limit check: `len(hits) <= self.limit` permits limit+1 requests per window (e.g. 101 when limit=100), weakening the security control; should be `len(hits) < self.limit`.

Unbounded memory growth / DoS (CWE-770): the _hits dict is only pruned by the manual sweep(); check() never evicts, so combined with the attacker-controlled XFF key (line 32) an adversary can create unlimited buckets via distinct X-Forwarded-For values and exhaust server memory.

No locking around the shared _hits / per-key list mutations (CWE-362): the prune→length-check→append sequence in check() is a non-atomic read-modify-write, so concurrent gateway requests can both pass the limit check before appending and exceed the limit (TOCTOU) or interleave list ops into a corrupt/erroring state.

Log injection / forging (CWE-117): the client key, which is fully attacker-controlled via X-Forwarded-For, is written to the warning log unsanitized, allowing newline/control-char injection of forged log entries; denial floods also amplify log volume.

All clients missing both headers collapse into a single shared "unknown" bucket, so one abusive header-less client can exhaust that quota and get every other anonymous client throttled (shared-bucket unfairness, A06).

hits.pop(0) is O(n) per removed element (list shift); pruning a window is up to O(n^2) — bounded small by limit so low impact, but a collections.deque with popleft() would be O(1) (deferring depth to the performance critic).
